answers.intake_q1: When did your back pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly your back pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury, trauma, or heavy lifting before the pain started?
answers.intake_q4: Any fever, chills, or unexplained weight loss since the pain started?
answers.intake_q5: Any pain radiating to your abdomen, groin, or legs?
answers.intake_q6: Have you noticed any changes in your bowel or bladder function since the pain started?
answers.intake_q7: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your back pain, and if so, what were they and did they help?
answers.intake_a7: I tried tylenol, but it didn't really help much
answers.intake_q8: Any history of kidney stones, urinary tract infections, or other kidney problems?
answers.intake_q9: Any shortness of breath, cough, or chest pain along with your back pain?
answers.intake_q10: Any history of chronic illnesses such as osteoporosis, cancer, or recent infections?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with left-sided lower rib cage back pain that started three weeks ago. She denies any recent injury, trauma, or heavy lifting prior to onset. There are no associated symptoms such as fever, chills, unexplained weight loss, pain radiating to the abdomen, groin, or legs, or changes in bowel or bladder function. She has tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) with minimal relief. There is no history of kidney stones, urinary tract infections, chronic illnesses, or recent infections. She denies shortness of breath, cough, or chest pain. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
The working diagnosis is mechanical back pain localized to the left lower rib cage area, likely musculoskeletal in origin given the absence of red flag symptoms or systemic signs. 

Differential: 
1. Musculoskeletal strain or sprain 
2. Intercostal muscle strain or rib dysfunction 
3. Early costovertebral joint arthritis or inflammation 
4. Less likely visceral causes such as referred pain from abdominal organs 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen for pain relief and inflammation reduction. 
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; patient currently has no known renal or liver disease. Avoid NSAIDs if any new contraindications develop. 
- Return precautions: Return promptly if she develops fever, chills, unexplained weight loss, worsening pain, new neurological symptoms, changes in bowel or bladder function, or if pain radiates to other areas. 
- Testing: No imaging or laboratory testing indicated at this time given absence of red flags; reassess if symptoms persist beyond expected recovery or worsen.

evidence.title: NIAMS: Living With Back Pain (patient-friendly NIH resource)
evidence.snippet: Acute back pain happens suddenly and usually lasts a few days to a few weeks. Back pain that lasts longer than 12 weeks is called chronic back pain.   Who Gets Back Pain? Image Anyone can have back pain. You may be more likely to have back pain because of the following: Age: Back pain is more common as you age. Fitness level: Back pain is more common if…

evidence.title: AAFP Choosing Wisely: Imaging for Low Back Pain (Red Flags)
evidence.snippet: Information Low back pain is one of the most common reasons for an outpatient visit.
